content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Welcome back to our Cursor series! In Part 2, we took our raw text data and converted it into high-dimensional vector embeddings using OpenAI’s API. But keeping those vectors floating in a raw list isn’t going to cut it. If your dataset scales to thousands or millions of documents, comparing a user’s query against every single vector using a brute-force loop turns into an operational nightmare.
To build a production-grade Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system, you need an infrastructure that can search through millions of vectors in milliseconds.
Enter FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search). Developed by Meta, FAISS is the industry standard for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors. In this installment, we will use Cursor to build a high-performance, production-ready Python vector search engine that handles indexing, disk persistence, metadata mapping, and noise filtering.
The Production Workflow
Moving past a simple toy demo means managing the entire lifecycle of a vector index. Order is critical here: we must normalize our geometric space before indexing, and we must keep our vector positions perfectly in sync with our document metadata when saving to disk.
1.Format & Normalize Vectors:
Convert your raw embeddings into a NumPy array with a strict float32 data type. Run an L2 normalization so our geometric distance math functions correctly.
